target.name: bicyclomahanimbine
target.motivation: Bicyclomahanimbine is a hexacyclic heptane [3,2,0] core carbazole alkaloid with potential pharmacological properties. Previous syntheses were time-consuming and low-yielding. The authors aim to develop a more efficient and sustainable synthesis using photoredox chemistry.

prior_syntheses.key_strategy: Sunlight irradiation of mahanimbine for 26.5 days

success_criteria.free_text: Expediting the synthesis within a significantly shortened timeframe and improving atom economics and environmental impact.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bonds forming the bicyclo[3.2.0]heptane system
key_disconnections.reasoning: Inspired by biosynthetic pathways and aiming for a sustainable approach using photoredox catalysis.
To construct the 2H-chromene ring on mahanimbine from 2-hydroxy-3-methylcarbazole and citral, improving upon Lewis-acid mediated cyclization.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-N bond forming the carbazole motif
key_disconnections.reasoning: Sustainable and cost-effective method to craft the carbazole motif from a biphenyl precursor.

key_disconnections.bond_description: C-C bond between two aryl rings
key_disconnections.reasoning: Established method for C-C bond formation.

retrosynthetic_strategy.rationale: The overall strategy is divergent, using mahanimbine as a precursor for various pyrano-[3,2-a]-carbazole alkaloids. The synthesis of bicyclomahanimbine specifically focuses on a formal [2+2]-cycloaddition from mahanimbine. The synthesis of mahanimbine itself starts from commercially available 4-bromo-2-methylanisole, building the carbazole core via Suzuki-Miyaura coupling and electrochemical C-N bond formation, followed by pyran annulation with citral.

thought.background: The synthesis begins with commercially available 4-bromo-2-methylanisole (13).
thought.gap: How to convert 4-bromo-2-methylanisole into a boronic acid for Suzuki coupling.
thought.decision: To form the boronic acid, 4-bromo-2-methylanisole (13) was treated with nBuLi for metal-halogen exchange, followed by quenching with B(OMe)3 and acidic work-up, expecting 4-methoxy-m-tolyl boronic acid (11).

evidence.text_span: Our experimental approach commenced by treating 4- bromo-2-methylanisole (13) with n BuLi for metal-halogen exchange, followed by quenching of the generated aryl lithium species with B (OMe) 3 and subsequent acidic work-up. This process yielded 4-methoxy m -tolyl boronic acid (11), which underwent a Pd(0)-catalyzed Suzuki–Miyaura coupling22 with readily available 2-bromonitrobenzene (12) to form compound (10).

thought.gap: How to form the C-C bond between the boronic acid and 2-bromonitrobenzene.
thought.decision: To form the C-C bond, 4-methoxy-m-tolyl boronic acid (11) was subjected to a Pd(0)-catalyzed Suzuki–Miyaura coupling with 2-bromonitrobenzene (12), expecting compound (10).

evidence.text_span: This process yielded 4-methoxy m -tolyl boronic acid (11), which underwent a Pd(0)-catalyzed Suzuki–Miyaura coupling22 with readily available 2-bromonitrobenzene (12) to form compound (10).

thought.background: Compound (10) with a nitro group was successfully synthesized.
thought.gap: How to reduce the nitro group to an amino group.
thought.decision: To reduce the nitro group, compound (10) was treated with Pd/C-occluded H2, expecting amino compound (14).

outcome.observation: The nitro group on (10) was fully reduced to an amino group, yielding compound (14).

evidence.text_span: Aer forming the C–C bond, the nitro group on (10) was fully reduced to an amino group with Pd/C-occluded H 2 (Scheme 2).

thought.background: Compound (14) with an amino group was obtained.
thought.gap: How to construct the carbazole scaffold via electrochemical oxidation.
thought.decision: To construct the carbazole scaffold, compound (14) was subjected to electrochemical oxidation using a constant potential protocol, expecting carbazole formation.

outcome.observation: This approach was unsuccessful, likely due to susceptibility towards side reactions.

evidence.text_span: Subsequently, we attempted electrochemical oxidation to construct the carbazole scaffold from compound (14). Due to their susceptibility towards side reactions,23 we implemented a constant potential protocol. However, this approach was unsuccessful.

thought.background: Direct electrochemical oxidation of compound (14) failed.
thought.gap: How to enable successful electrochemical C-N bond formation for the carbazole scaffold.
thought.decision: To facilitate the dehydrogenative electrochemical reaction, the amino group of compound (14) was protected with an acetyl group, forming compound (15), expecting successful cyclization to carbazole (16).

outcome.observation: Protection of the amino group with an acetyl group was successful, yielding compound (15).

evidence.text_span: Notably, when the amino group was protected with an acetyl group (as in 15), the desired C–N bond formation (see, carbazole 16) occurred with a yield of 73% (r : r = 3 : 1) (Scheme 3) [for detailed optimization, see the ESI† for details].

thought.background: The amino group of compound (14) was protected as an acetyl group, yielding compound (15).
thought.gap: How to achieve the desired C-N bond formation to construct the carbazole scaffold.
thought.decision: To construct the carbazole scaffold, compound (15) was subjected to dehydrogenative electrochemical reaction, expecting carbazole (16).

outcome.observation: Desired C–N bond formation occurred, yielding carbazole (16).

evidence.text_span: Notably, when the amino group was protected with an acetyl group (as in 15), the desired C–N bond formation (see, carbazole 16) occurred with a yield of 73% (r : r = 3 : 1) (Scheme 3) [for detailed optimization, see the ESI† for details].

thought.background: Carbazole (16) with an acetyl group and a methoxy group was obtained.
thought.gap: How to remove the acetyl protecting group and cleave the phenolic ether to obtain 2-hydroxy-3-methylcarbazole (9).
thought.decision: To obtain the biological carbazole precursor, carbazole (16) was subjected to alkaline hydrolysis of the amide, followed by phenolic ether cleavage using BBr3, expecting 2-hydroxy-3-methylcarbazole (9).

evidence.text_span: Having ample quantities of carbazole (16) available, we proceeded with alkaline hydrolysis of amide followed by phenolic ether cleavage using BBr 3 . These set of reactions provided the biological carbazole precursor, 2-hydroxy-3- methylcarbazole (9).

thought.gap: How to construct the 2H-chromene motif to form mahanimbine (1).
thought.decision: To construct the 2H-chromene motif, 2-hydroxy-3-methylcarbazole (9) was reacted with citral (8) in pyridine under reflux conditions, expecting mahanimbine (1).

outcome.observation: Mahanimbine (1) was obtained with an impressive yield.

evidence.text_span: Aer exhaustive optimization, we found that reuxing the phenolic compound (9) in pyridine solvent in the presence of 10 equiv. citral (8) yielded mahanimbine (1) with an impressive yield of 76% (Scheme 4).

thought.background: Mahanimbine (1) was synthesized. Previous attempts to convert mahanimbine to bicyclomahanimbine (2) were low yielding or time-consuming.
thought.gap: How to expedite the synthesis of bicyclomahanimbine (2) from mahanimbine (1) with improved yield.
thought.decision: To synthesize bicyclomahanimbine (2), mahanimbine (1) was subjected to a formal [2+2]-cycloaddition under visible light irradiation without a catalyst, expecting the product.

outcome.observation: No reaction was observed under these conditions.

evidence.text_span: Entry 3: — Et2O 500 W Na-lamp 3 d NR

thought.background: Direct visible light irradiation of mahanimbine (1) without a catalyst failed to produce bicyclomahanimbine (2).
thought.gap: How to achieve the formal [2+2]-cycloaddition with improved yield and reduced time.
thought.decision: To synthesize bicyclomahanimbine (2), mahanimbine (1) was subjected to a formal [2+2]-cycloaddition using Cu(OTf)2 as a catalyst under visible light irradiation, expecting the product with high yield.

outcome.observation: Bicyclomahanimbine (2) was obtained with a high yield in a significantly shortened timeframe.

evidence.text_span: The most successful approach involved using Cu(OTf) 2 as a catalyst (10 mol%) in diethyl ether solvent under the irradiation of a 500 W Na-lamp, achieving a maximum yield of 87%.

Overall Yield.interpretation: Overall yield for bicyclomahanimbine is not explicitly stated, but individual step yields are high.

Longest Linear Steps.interpretation: Number of steps in the longest linear sequence to bicyclomahanimbine.

Total Steps.interpretation: Total number of distinct chemical transformations performed.

Failed Attempts Count.interpretation: Number of explicitly reported failed attempts.

validation.comparison_to_prior: The synthesis of bicyclomahanimbine achieved an 87% yield in 10 hours, significantly improving upon previous methods which yielded 45% after 26.5 days (Knölker group) or 42% (Tan group).
validation.significance: This work demonstrates a highly efficient and sustainable total synthesis of bicyclomahanimbine using photoredox catalysis, addressing the drawbacks of previous reports regarding reaction time and yield. The use of Cu(OTf)2 in the key [2+2]-cycloaddition is a significant advancement.

source.title: Total synthesis of bicyclomahanimbine by Cu(ii)-promoted photoredox process.
source.authors: Dangar S, Roy T, Noskar S, Bisai A.
